{"step_by_step_thinking": "The newborn presents with jaundice within 16 hours of birth, which is pathologic and suggests hemolytic disease. The key laboratory findings include a total bilirubin of 11.3 mg/dL (unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia, as direct bilirubin is only 0.3 mg/dL), elevated reticulocytes (9%), and signs of hemolysis such as hepatosplenomegaly (palpable liver and spleen). The maternal blood group is O, Rh-negative, and the fetal blood group is B, Rh-negative. The maternal anti-Rh antibody titer is positive, indicating the mother has developed antibodies against Rh-positive fetal red blood cells.

Rh incompatibility is a common cause of hemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) in Rh-negative mothers with Rh-positive fetuses. However, in this case, the fetus is Rh-negative, so anti-D antibodies would not be expected to cause hemolysis. Therefore, option B (Anti-D antibodies) is not the correct answer.

The fetus has blood group B, and the mother is group O. Mothers with blood group O naturally produce anti-A and anti-B antibodies, which are IgG and can cross the placenta. This leads to hemolysis of fetal red blood cells with blood group B. This is known as ABO incompatibility. The presence of anti-B antibodies in the mother is consistent with this scenario. Although the direct Coombs test (DAT) is typically negative in ABO incompatibility (as noted in the documents, DAT is a poor positive predictor in ABO HDFN), the clinical picture—early jaundice, elevated bilirubin, anemia, and splenomegaly—fits ABO incompatibility.

Option A (RBC sickling) is less likely because the newborn's brother has sickle cell disease, but the newborn is not described as having any signs of sickling (e.g., pallor, pain, deformities), and the laboratory findings (normal hemoglobin, no evidence of hemolysis from membrane defects) do not support this.

Option C (Biliary duct malformation) would present with conjugated hyperbilirubinemia (elevated direct bilirubin), which is not the case here (direct bilirubin is only 0.3 mg/dL). Therefore, this is not the cause.

Thus, the most likely cause is anti-B antibodies due to ABO incompatibility between the mother (O) and the fetus (B).", "answer_choice": "D"}
